title: The British Prime Minister and Our Indian Prime Minister!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The OCR-derived text corrupted `ஈழத் தமிழர்கள்`, Australian Senator Lee Rhiannon's name, two printed separators, and the closing verb `கர்ஜித்தாரா?`. These readings were verified from the source pages and restored in the derived Tamil copy. Long English passages printed in the source are retained as quotations; their original wording is not treated as Tamil OCR corruption. The canonical source files remain unchanged.
paragraphs: After Tamils across the world, and countries such as Canada, Britain, Mauritius, Trinidad and Australia, raised many concerns, the Commonwealth meeting in Sri Lanka has now concluded. Its one distinction was the pressure it placed upon the Sri Lankan President who hosted it, and the international attention received by British Prime Minister David Cameron.

David Cameron has found a place in the hearts of Eelam Tamils living in Sri Lanka and throughout the world, and indeed in the hearts of all Tamils in Tamil Nadu. Our Indian Prime Minister too could have earned this praise and honour. Do you know when?

Tamil Nadu would have been immersed in an ocean of joy had India announced that neither the Prime Minister nor even a “splinter” from India would attend the Commonwealth meeting, and had the delegation led by External Affairs Minister Salman Khurshid also stayed away in condemnation of Sri Lanka's cruelties against Eelam Tamils, its human-rights violations, war crimes and genocide. Tamils would have praised it with one voice, and our Prime Minister too would have attracted international attention.

Instead, the Indian Prime Minister adopted an awkward halfway position, seemingly trying to show his head to the Sri Lankan President and his tail to the Tamils: he did not go to Sri Lanka, but sent a Union minister.

Even then, he could have issued an explicit statement that he was staying away to condemn the Sri Lankan government's atrocities against Eelam Tamils. Or Salman Khurshid could have done what the British Prime Minister did: visited the areas where Eelam Tamils live, offered India's consolation to people who continue to endure only suffering, and carried their heart-rending condition to the Sri Lankan President.

He could have demanded an immediate inquiry into the continuing atrocities committed by the Sri Lankan military against Eelam Tamils, warning that otherwise India would take the matter to the United Nations and Human Rights Council and move a resolution. Had he said even this much, every honour now received by Cameron would have come to the Indian government. The global Tamil community would have thanked India for coming forward to support us.
At yesterday's TESO meeting in Anna Arivalayam, the first resolution condemning India's conduct stated:

> When even the Prime Minister of Britain, a country without such close ties to the Eelam Tamil issue, has expressed views this strongly, it cannot in any way be justified that India, a fraternal country with the right, duty and responsibility truly to speak for and aid Eelam Tamils, disregarded the united appeal of the Tamil people and sent External Affairs Minister Salman Khurshid to attend that meeting.
The second resolution declared firmly:

> Understanding the openly expressed views of leaders from Britain and several other countries, and the feelings of Tamils throughout the world, the Union government must at least now demand an international investigation into the human-rights violations, war crimes and genocide committed in Sri Lanka, and must pass a resolution in the Indian Parliament.
Do you know what newspapers in India and abroad wrote about India's conduct? Here are one or two examples.
*The New York Times* wrote in an editorial appearing in its Thursday edition:

> Sri Lanka [has committed] “serious human rights violations” and [failed] “to provide accountability for war-time abuses.” Under the Rajapaksa Government, journalists have been systematically threatened, harassed, killed or forced into exile in what amounts to a war on the press. Colombo did everything it could to sabotage a free and fair outcome in the Northern elections, including publishing a fake edition of the local newspaper *Uthayan*. > > The leaders attending the Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting [must] insist that Colombo show meaningful progress in addressing human rights abuses, abiding by international standards, and restore constitutional protection for freedom of expression.
*The Hindu* said in an editorial:

> Manmohan Singh's decision not to attend the summit has brought no political or diplomatic advantage. By comparison, British Prime Minister David Cameron showed far more nous. He silenced political opposition to his participation by using the occasion to publicly criticise Sri Lanka's rights record, issuing an ultimatum to President Rajapaksa to institute by March a credible inquiry into allegations of rights violations. > > How useful such ultimatums are as a diplomatic strategy is questionable, going by the Sri Lankan leader's defiant stance, but the British Prime Minister achieved his stated aim of “focus(ing) the eyes of the world on Sri Lanka.” He also did what the Indian Prime Minister should have done: visited Jaffna, connecting with the Tamil people and their problems first-hand. > > New Delhi will face its next Sri Lanka test in March 2014 at the U.N. Human Rights Council, but its timing right before the elections means it may blunder through again.
And here is a voice from the Australian Parliament:

> Lee Rhiannon, a Greens Party Senator from New South Wales, during a debate in the Australian Parliament following her return from Sri Lanka on the eve of the Commonwealth Meeting, said that the situation in the North East under Mahinda Rajapakse regime is dire, that the Sri Lanka army's involvement in civilian life is pervasive and Australia is turning a blind eye to credible allegations of continuing torture, rape and crimes against humanity reported by respected Rights groups and by several video documentaries from Channel-4. > > Rhiannon pointed out the increasingly voiced broad consensus from several countries for an international investigations into the war-crimes by the Rajapakse regime, and praised the CHOGM boycott of several Prime Ministers in spotlighting the Sri Lanka's continuing dismal rights record and lack of accountability for war-crimes. She added a Catholic father who has documented the details of more than 2000 people disappeared after being arrested alive had told her that crimes that have been committed and those that continue to be perpetrated amount to genecide against the Tamils.
*The New Indian Express* said in an editorial:
> Rajapaksa needs to remember that nobody trusts his Government when it says there were no violations and the end of the war has brought peace and tranquility to Sri Lanka.
Under the heading “The Opportunity That Slipped Away,” *Dinamani* wrote:

> The Prime Ministers of Canada and Mauritius openly announced that they were boycotting the Commonwealth meeting in condemnation of the genocide in Sri Lanka and the human-rights violations committed during the war against the Liberation Tigers. The Indian Prime Minister issued no such statement. Instead, he sent the External Affairs Minister in his place, showing his head to Tamil Nadu's political parties and his tail to the Sri Lankan government, and slipped away. > > He should either have acted courageously like the other two Prime Ministers who boycotted the meeting, condemning Sri Lanka's human-rights violations and demanding an international investigation, or he should have gone to Sri Lanka despite domestic opposition. Like British Prime Minister David Cameron, he should have visited Jaffna, Mullaitivu and Trincomalee, heard the grievances of the people there, raised those issues both at the Commonwealth meeting in Colombo and directly with Sri Lankan President Rajapaksa, and made them a subject of discussion.
The *Dinamani* editorial concluded with this advice to President Rajapaksa: “Sri Lanka does not need a separate Foreign Minister. Salman Khurshid is performing that work efficiently.”

Even China, which gives Sri Lanka extensive assistance as a friendly country, said through Foreign Ministry spokesperson Qin Gang: “Compared with economic and social development in various countries, the protection of human rights in Sri Lanka is different. Sri Lanka must take measures to protect and promote human rights.” Yet did our External Affairs Minister, who went to Sri Lanka, open his mouth?
I have gathered here the views expressed by many people about the Rajapaksa government's actions, the Commonwealth meeting, and India's approach.

British Prime Minister David Cameron earned the united praise of the global Tamil community by visiting the devastated areas of Jaffna on the very first day of the meeting, 15 November 2013. He met affected Eelam Tamil people personally and heard all their grievances. Only then did he return to Colombo and meet President Mahinda Rajapaksa that night.
Cameron was the first foreign Prime Minister to visit Jaffna after Sri Lanka gained independence from Britain in 1948. In an interview on 16 November 2013, he said:

> Sri Lanka must take steps by March to conduct its own transparent inquiry into events during the final phase of the war. If it misses the deadline, I will approach the UN Human Rights Council and press for an independent investigation. An international investigation is necessary to learn what happened in the safe zones where civilians were brought and housed after being told they would be protected from attack.
He further said:

> I went to Jaffna with several international organisations to bring the chilling events that occurred there into the light and show them to the world. I did so as a member of the Commonwealth family.

The Tamil community expected India and the Indian Prime Minister to do what the British Prime Minister did. But does India show the concern and compassion for the Tamil community that the British Prime Minister has shown?

If the Prime Minister truly stayed away from the meeting in accordance with the wishes of Tamil Nadu's people, could he not have openly stated that reason in his letter to the Sri Lankan President? Could he not have written that particular atrocities had been committed against Eelam Tamils, that a transparent inquiry must be held, and that the truth must become known to the world?

Ignoring every Tamil demand, the Indian External Affairs Minister went to Sri Lanka to attend in the Prime Minister's place. What did he do there? On the first day itself, the British Prime Minister rushed to Jaffna, met the affected Tamil people and offered consolation. Did our minister do the same? Did he, like Cameron, call publicly for an investigation? Did he roar at the Commonwealth meeting against the atrocities committed in Sri Lanka? Did he set out the reasons why the Prime Minister had not attended?
He did nothing. Why should Salman care? After all, those who were killed were only our Tamil people!
With affection,
M. K.
20 November 2013
